infobox.key: Antonio de Padua María Severino López de Santa Anna y Pérez de Lebrón
infobox.value: (21 February 1794 – 21 June 1876),[[1]](#cite_note-Handbook-1) often known as **Santa Anna**,[[2]](#cite_note-2) was a Mexican general, politician, and *[caudillo](/wiki/Caudillo "Caudillo")*[[3]](#cite_note-3) who served as the 8th [president of Mexico](/wiki/President_of_Mexico "President of Mexico") on multiple occasions between 1833 and 1855. He also served as [vice president of Mexico](/wiki/List_of_vice_presidents_of_Mexico "List of vice presidents of Mexico") from 1837 to 1839. He was a controversial and pivotal figure in Mexican politics during the 19th century, to the point that he has been called an "uncrowned monarch",[[4]](#cite_note-4) and historians often refer to the three decades after Mexican independence as the "Age of Santa Anna".[[5]](#cite_note-5)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

President of Mexico many times, 1833 to 1855

In this [Spanish name](/wiki/Spanish_name "Spanish name"), the first or paternal [surname](/wiki/Surname "Surname") is  *López de Santa Anna* and the second or maternal family name is  *Pérez de Lebrón*.

**Antonio de Padua María Severino López de Santa Anna y Pérez de Lebrón** (21 February 1794 – 21 June 1876),[[1]](#cite_note-Handbook-1) often known as **Santa Anna**,[[2]](#cite_note-2) was a Mexican general, politician, and *[caudillo](/wiki/Caudillo "Caudillo")*[[3]](#cite_note-3) who served as the 8th [president of Mexico](/wiki/President_of_Mexico "President of Mexico") on multiple occasions between 1833 and 1855. He also served as [vice president of Mexico](/wiki/List_of_vice_presidents_of_Mexico "List of vice presidents of Mexico") from 1837 to 1839. He was a controversial and pivotal figure in Mexican politics during the 19th century, to the point that he has been called an "uncrowned monarch",[[4]](#cite_note-4) and historians often refer to the three decades after Mexican independence as the "Age of Santa Anna".[[5]](#cite_note-5)

Santa Anna was in charge of the garrison at [Veracruz](/wiki/Veracruz "Veracruz") at the time [Mexico won independence](/wiki/Mexican_War_of_Independence "Mexican War of Independence") in 1821. He would go on to play a notable role in the fall of the [First Mexican Empire](/wiki/First_Mexican_Empire "First Mexican Empire"), the fall of the [First Mexican Republic](/wiki/First_Mexican_Republic "First Mexican Republic"), the promulgation of the [Constitution of 1835](/wiki/Siete_Leyes "Siete Leyes"), the establishment of the [Centralist Republic of Mexico](/wiki/Centralist_Republic_of_Mexico "Centralist Republic of Mexico"), the [Texas Revolution](/wiki/Texas_Revolution "Texas Revolution"), the [Pastry War](/wiki/Pastry_War "Pastry War"), the promulgation of the [Constitution of 1843](/wiki/Bases_Org%C3%A1nicas "Bases Orgánicas"), and the [Mexican–American War](/wiki/Mexican%E2%80%93American_War "Mexican–American War"). He became well known in the United States due to his role in the Texas Revolution and in the Mexican–American War.

Throughout his political career, Santa Anna was known for switching sides in the recurring conflict between the [Liberal Party](/wiki/Liberal_Party_(Mexico) "Liberal Party (Mexico)") and the [Conservative Party](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(Mexico) "Conservative Party (Mexico)"). He managed to play a prominent role in both discarding the liberal [Constitution of 1824](/wiki/1824_Constitution_of_Mexico "1824 Constitution of Mexico") in 1835 and in restoring it in 1847. He came to power as a liberal twice in 1832 and in 1847 respectively, both times sharing power with the liberal statesman [Valentín Gómez Farías](/wiki/Valent%C3%ADn_G%C3%B3mez_Far%C3%ADas "Valentín Gómez Farías"), and both times Santa Anna overthrew Gómez Farías after switching sides to the conservatives. Santa Anna was also known for his ostentatious and [dictatorial](/wiki/Dictator "Dictator") style of rule, making use of the military to dissolve [Congress](/wiki/Congress_of_the_Union "Congress of the Union") multiple times and referring to himself by the honorific title of *His Most Serene Highness*.

His intermittent periods of rule, which lasted from 1832 to 1853, witnessed the [loss of Texas](/wiki/Texas_annexation "Texas annexation"), a series of military failures during the Mexican–American War, and the ensuing [Mexican Cession](/wiki/Mexican_Cession "Mexican Cession"). His leadership in the war and his willingness to fight to the bitter end prolonged that conflict: "more than any other single person it was Santa Anna who denied [Polk](/wiki/James_K._Polk "James K. Polk")'s dream of a short war."[[6]](#cite_note-6) Even after the war was over, Santa Anna continued to cede national territory to the Americans through the [Gadsden Purchase](/wiki/Gadsden_Purchase "Gadsden Purchase") in 1853.

After he was overthrown and exiled in 1855 through the liberal [Plan of Ayutla](/wiki/Plan_of_Ayutla "Plan of Ayutla"), Santa Anna began to fade into the background in Mexican politics even as the nation entered the decisive period of the [Reform War](/wiki/Reform_War "Reform War"), the [Second French Intervention in Mexico](/wiki/Second_French_Intervention_in_Mexico "Second French Intervention in Mexico"), and the establishment of the [Second Mexican Empire](/wiki/Second_Mexican_Empire "Second Mexican Empire"). An elderly Santa Anna was allowed to return to the nation by President [Sebastián Lerdo de Tejada](/wiki/Sebasti%C3%A1n_Lerdo_de_Tejada "Sebastián Lerdo de Tejada") in 1874, and he died in relative obscurity in 1876.

Historians debate the exact number of his presidencies, as he would often share power and make use of [puppet rulers](/wiki/Puppet_ruler "Puppet ruler"); biographer Will Fowler gives the figure of six terms[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFowler2009xxi-7) while the [Texas State Historical Association](/wiki/Texas_State_Historical_Association "Texas State Historical Association") claims five.[[1]](#cite_note-Handbook-1) Historian of Latin America, Alexander Dawson, counts eleven times that Santa Anna assumed the presidency, often for short periods.[[8]](#cite_note-8) The [University of Texas](/wiki/University_of_Texas_at_Austin "University of Texas at Austin") Libraries cites the same figure of eleven times, but adds Santa Anna was only president for six years due to short terms.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

Santa Anna's legacy has subsequently come to be viewed as profoundly negative, with historians and many Mexicans ranking him as "the principal inhabitant even today of Mexico's black pantheon of those who failed the nation".[[10]](#cite_note-10) He is considered one of the most unpopular and controversial Mexican presidents of the 19th century.

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Antonio_L%C3%B3pez_de_Santa_Anna&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

Antonio de Padua María Severino López de Santa Anna y Pérez de Lebrón was born in [Xalapa](/wiki/Xalapa "Xalapa"), [Veracruz](/wiki/Veracruz "Veracruz"), [Nueva España](/wiki/New_Spain "New Spain") (New Spain), on 21 February 1794 into a respected Spanish family. He was named after his father, [licenciado](/wiki/Licentiate_(degree)#Mexico "Licentiate (degree)") Antonio López de Santa Anna y Pérez (born 1761), a university graduate and a lawyer; his mother was Manuela Pérez de Lebrón y Cortés (died 1814).[[11]](#cite_note-11)

Santa Anna's family prospered in Veracruz, where the merchant class dominated politics. His paternal uncle, Ángel López de Santa Anna, was a public clerk (*escribano*) and became aggrieved when the town council of Veracruz prevented him from moving to [Mexico City](/wiki/Mexico_City "Mexico City") to advance his career. Since the late 18th-century [Bourbon Reforms](/wiki/Bourbon_Reforms "Bourbon Reforms"), the [Spanish crown](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Spain "Monarchy of Spain") had favored peninsular-born Spaniards over American-born; young Santa Anna's family was affected by the growing disgruntlement of [creoles](/wiki/Criollo_people "Criollo people") whose upward mobility was thwarted.[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)

Santa Anna's mother favored her son's choice of a military career, supporting his desire to join the [Spanish Army](/wiki/Spanish_Army "Spanish Army"), rather than be a shopkeeper as his father preferred. His mother's friendly relationship with the *intendant* (governor) of Veracruz secured Santa Anna's military appointment despite the fact that he was underage. His parents' marriage produced seven children, four sisters and two brothers, and Santa Anna was close to his sister Francisca and brother Manuel, who also joined the army.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Antonio_L%C3%B3pez_de_Santa_Anna&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career")]

Santa Anna's origins on Mexico's [eastern coast](/wiki/Gulf_Coast_of_Mexico "Gulf Coast of Mexico") had important ramifications for his military career, as he had developed immunity from [yellow fever](/wiki/Yellow_fever "Yellow fever"), endemic to the region. The port of Veracruz and environs were known to be unhealthy for those not native to the region,[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16) so he had a personal strategic advantage against military officers from elsewhere. Being an officer in a time of war was a way that a provincial, middle-class man could vault from obscurity to a position of leadership. Santa Anna distinguished himself in battle, a path that led him to a national political career.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

Santa Anna's provincial origins made him uncomfortable in the halls of power in Mexico City, which were dominated by cliques of elite men, and thus he frequently made retreats to his base in Veracruz. He cultivated contact with ordinary Mexican men and pursued entertainments such as [cockfighting](/wiki/Cockfighting "Cockfighting"). Over his career, Santa Anna was a populist *[caudillo](/wiki/Caudillo "Caudillo")*, a [strongman](/wiki/Strongman "Strongman") wielding both military and political power, similar to others who emerged in the wake of [Spanish American wars of independence](/wiki/Spanish_American_wars_of_independence "Spanish American wars of independence").[[18]](#cite_note-18)

### War of Independence, 1810–1821

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Antonio_L%C3%B3pez_de_Santa_Anna&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: War of Independence, 1810–1821")]

Santa Anna's early military career during the [Mexican War of Independence](/wiki/Mexican_War_of_Independence "Mexican War of Independence"), which entailed fighting the insurgency before switching sides against the crown, presaged his many shifts in allegiance during his later political career. In June 1810, the 16-year-old Santa Anna joined the Fijo de Veracruz infantry regiment.[[19]](#cite_note-19) In September of that year, secular cleric [Miguel Hidalgo y Costilla](/wiki/Miguel_Hidalgo_y_Costilla "Miguel Hidalgo y Costilla") sparked a spontaneous mass uprising in the [Bajío](/wiki/Baj%C3%ADo "Bajío"), Mexico's rich agricultural area. Although some creole elites had chafed as their upward mobility had been thwarted by the Bourbon Reforms, the Hidalgo Revolt saw most creoles favoring continued crown rule. In particular, Santa Anna's family "saw themselves as aligned to the peninsular elite, whom they served, and were in turn recognized as belonging".[[20]](#cite_note-20)

Initially, Santa Anna, like most creole military officers, fought for the crown against the mixed-raced insurgents for independence; his commanding officer was Colonel [José Joaquín de Arredondo](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Joaqu%C3%ADn_de_Arredondo "José Joaquín de Arredondo"). In 1811 he was wounded in the left hand by an arrow while fighting in the town of Amoladeras, in the intendancy (administrative district) of [San Luis Potosí](/wiki/San_Luis_Potos%C3%AD "San Luis Potosí"). In 1813 he served in [Texas](/wiki/Spanish_Texas "Spanish Texas") against the [Gutiérrez–Magee Expedition](/wiki/Guti%C3%A9rrez%E2%80%93Magee_Expedition "Gutiérrez–Magee Expedition") and at the [Battle of Medina](/wiki/Battle_of_Medina "Battle of Medina"), in which he was cited for bravery. Santa Anna was promoted quickly; he became a second lieutenant in February 1812 and first lieutenant before the end of that year. During the initial rebellion, the young officer witnessed Arredondo's fierce [counterinsurgency](/wiki/Counterinsurgency "Counterinsurgency") policy of mass executions. The early fighting against the rebels gave way to [guerrilla warfare](/wiki/Guerrilla_warfare "Guerrilla warfare") and a military [stalemate](/wiki/Stalemate "Stalemate").[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFowler200927-21)

When royalist officer [Agustín de Iturbide](/wiki/Agust%C3%ADn_de_Iturbide "Agustín de Iturbide") switched sides in 1821 and allied with insurgent [Vicente Guerrero](/wiki/Vicente_Guerrero "Vicente Guerrero"), fighting for independence under the [Plan of Iguala](/wiki/Plan_of_Iguala "Plan of Iguala"), Santa Anna also joined the fight for independence. Political developments in Spain, where liberals had ousted King [Ferdinand VII](/wiki/Ferdinand_VII_of_Spain "Ferdinand VII of Spain") and began implementing the Spanish liberal [constitution of 1812](/wiki/Spanish_Constitution_of_1812 "Spanish Constitution of 1812"), made many elites in Mexico reconsider their options.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

### Rebellion against the Mexican Empire of Iturbide, 1822–1823

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Antonio_L%C3%B3pez_de_Santa_Anna&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Rebellion against the Mexican Empire of Iturbide, 1822–1823")]

Further information: [Casa Mata Plan Revolution](/wiki/Casa_Mata_Plan_Revolution "Casa Mata Plan Revolution")

Iturbide, now [Emperor](/wiki/Emperor_of_Mexico "Emperor of Mexico") Augustin I, rewarded Santa Anna with the command of the vital port of Veracruz, the gateway from the [Gulf of Mexico](/wiki/Gulf_of_Mexico "Gulf of Mexico") to the rest of the nation and site of a [customs house](/wiki/Custom_house "Custom house"). However, Iturbide subsequently removed Santa Anna from the post, prompting Santa Anna to rise in rebellion in December 1822 against Iturbide. He already had significant power in his home region of Veracruz, and "he was well along the path to becoming the regional *caudillo*."[[23]](#cite_note-23) Santa Anna claimed in his [Plan of Veracruz](/wiki/Plan_of_Veracruz_(1822) "Plan of Veracruz (1822)") that he rebelled because Iturbide had dissolved the Constituent Congress. He also promised to support free trade with Spain, an important principle for his home region of Veracruz.[[24]](#cite_note-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25)

Although Santa Anna's initial rebellion was important, Iturbide had loyal military men who were able to hold their own against the rebels in Veracruz. However, former insurgent leaders Guerrero and [Nicolás Bravo](/wiki/Nicol%C3%A1s_Bravo "Nicolás Bravo"), who had supported Iturbide's Plan de Iguala, returned to their base in southern Mexico and raised a rebellion against Iturbide. The commander of imperial forces in Veracruz, who had fought against the rebels, changed sides and joined the rebels. The new coalition proclaimed the [Plan of Casa Mata](/wiki/Plan_of_Casa_Mata "Plan of Casa Mata"), which called for the end of the monarchy, restoration of the Constituent Congress, and creation of a [republic](/wiki/Republic "Republic") and a [federal system](/wiki/Federal_system "Federal system").[[26]](#cite_note-26)

No longer the main player in the movement against Iturbide or the creation of new political arrangements, Santa Anna sought to regain his position as a leader and marched forces to [Tampico](/wiki/Tampico "Tampico"), then to San Luis Potosí, proclaiming his role as the "protector of the federation". Representatives from San Luis Potosí and other north-central regions, such as [Michoacán](/wiki/Michoac%C3%A1n "Michoacán"), [Querétaro](/wiki/Quer%C3%A9taro "Querétaro"), and [Guanajuato](/wiki/Guanajuato "Guanajuato"), met to decide their own position towards the federation. Santa Anna pledged his military forces to the protection of these key areas. "He attempted, in other words, to co-opt the movement, the first of many examples in his long career where he placed himself as the head of a generalized movement so it would become an instrument of his advancement."[[27]](#cite_note-27)

### Santa Anna and the early Mexican Republic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Antonio_L%C3%B3pez_de_Santa_Anna&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Santa Anna and the early Mexican Republic")]

In May 1823, following Iturbide's [abdication](/wiki/Abdication "Abdication") as emperor in March, Santa Anna was sent to command in [Yucatán](/wiki/Yucat%C3%A1n "Yucatán"). At the time, Yucatán's capital of [Mérida](/wiki/M%C3%A9rida,_Yucat%C3%A1n "Mérida, Yucatán") and the port city of [Campeche](/wiki/Campeche_City "Campeche City") were in conflict. Yucatán's closest trade partner was Cuba, a Spanish colony. Santa Anna took it upon himself to plan a landing force from Yucatán in Cuba, which he envisioned would result in Cuban colonists welcoming their "liberators", most especially himself. One thousand Mexicans were already on ships to sail to Cuba when word came that the Spanish were reinforcing their colony, so the invasion was called off.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

Former insurgent general [Guadalupe Victoria](/wiki/Guadalupe_Victoria "Guadalupe Victoria"), a liberal federalist, became the first president of the [Mexican republic](/wiki/First_Mexican_Republic "First Mexican Republic") in 1824, following the creation of the [constitution of 1824](/wiki/1824_Constitution_of_Mexico "1824 Constitution of Mexico"). Victoria came to the presidency with little factional conflict, and served out his entire four-year term. However, the [election of 1828](/wiki/1828_Mexican_general_election "1828 Mexican general election") was quite different, with considerable political conflict in which Santa Anna became involved.

Even before the election, there was unrest in Mexico, with some conservatives affiliated with the [Scottish Rite](/wiki/Scottish_Rite "Scottish Rite") [Freemasons](/wiki/Freemasonry "Freemasonry") plotting rebellion. The so-called Montaño rebellion in December 1827 called for the prohibition of [secret societies](/wiki/Secret_society "Secret society"), implicitly meaning liberal [York Rite](/wiki/York_Rite "York Rite") Freemasons, and the expulsion of U.S. diplomat [Joel Roberts Poinsett](/wiki/Joel_Roberts_Poinsett "Joel Roberts Poinsett"), a promoter of federal republicanism. Although Santa Anna was believed to be a supporter of the Scottish Rite conservatives, and Santa Anna was himself a member of the Scottish Rite,[[29]](#cite_note-29)[[30]](#cite_note-30) in the Montaño rebellion he eventually threw his support to the liberals. In his home state of Veracruz, the governor had thrown his support to the rebels, and in the aftermath of the rebellion's failure, Santa Anna as vice-governor stepped into the governorship.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

In the 1828 election, Santa Anna supported Guerrero, who was a candidate for the presidency. Another important liberal, [Lorenzo de Zavala](/wiki/Lorenzo_de_Zavala "Lorenzo de Zavala"), also supported Guerrero. However, conservative [Manuel Gómez Pedraza](/wiki/Manuel_G%C3%B3mez_Pedraza "Manuel Gómez Pedraza") won the indirect elections for the presidency, with Guerrero coming in second. Even before all the votes had been counted, Santa Anna raised a rebellion and called for the nullification of the election results, as well for a new law expelling Spanish nationals who he believed to have been in league with the conservatives. The rebellion initially had few supporters, although southern Mexican leader [Juan Álvarez](/wiki/Juan_%C3%81lvarez "Juan Álvarez") soon joined Santa Anna, while Zavala, under threat of arrest by the conservative [Senate](/wiki/Senate_of_the_Republic_(Mexico) "Senate of the Republic (Mexico)"), fled to the mountains and organized his own rebellion. Zavala brought the fighting into Mexico City, with his supporters seizing an armory, the *Acordada*. President-elect Gómez Pedraza resigned and soon after went into [exile](/wiki/Exile "Exile"), clearing the way for Guerrero to assume office. Santa Anna gained prominence for his role in Gómez Pedraza's ouster, and was lauded as a defender of federalism and democracy.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5c/Acci%C3%B3n_militar_en_Pueblo_Viejo_%28Batalla_de_Tampico%2C_11-9-1829%29%2C_Carlos_Par%C3%ADs_%281820_-_1835%29.png/250px-Acci%C3%B3n_militar_en_Pueblo_Viejo_%28Batalla_de_Tampico%2C_11-9-1829%29%2C_Carlos_Par%C3%ADs_%281820_-_1835%29.png)](/wiki/File:Acci%C3%B3n_militar_en_Pueblo_Viejo_(Batalla_de_Tampico,_11-9-1829),_Carlos_Par%C3%ADs_(1820_-_1835).png)

1835 painting of the [Battle of Tampico](/wiki/Battle_of_Tampico_(1829) "Battle of Tampico (1829)")

In 1829, Spain made a [final attempt](/wiki/Battle_of_Tampico_(1829) "Battle of Tampico (1829)") to retake Mexico, invading Tampico with a force of 2,600 troops. Santa Anna marched against the [Barradas Expedition](/wiki/Isidro_Barradas "Isidro Barradas") with a much smaller force and defeated the Spaniards, many of whom were suffering from yellow fever. The defeat of the Spanish Army not only firmly established Santa Anna as a national hero but also consolidated the independence of the new Mexican republic. From this point forward, Santa Anna styled himself the "Victor of Tampico" and the "Savior of the Patria". His main act of self-promotion was to call himself the "[Napoleon](/wiki/Napoleon_Bonaparte "Napoleon Bonaparte") of the West".

Three months later, in December 1829, Vice-president [Anastasio Bustamante](/wiki/Anastasio_Bustamante "Anastasio Bustamante"), a conservative, mounted a successful *[coup d'etat](/wiki/Coup_d%27etat "Coup d'etat")* against President Guerrero, who left Mexico City to lead a counter-rebellion in the south. Guerrero was captured and executed after a summary trial in 1831, which shocked the nation.[[33]](#cite_note-33) In 1832, Santa Anna seized the customs revenues from Veracruz and declared himself in rebellion against Bustamante. The bloody conflict ended with Santa Anna forcing the resignation of Bustamante's cabinet, and an agreement was brokered for new elections in 1833.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

### "Absentee President", 1833–1835

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Antonio_L%C3%B3pez_de_Santa_Anna&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: \"Absentee President\", 1833–1835")]

See also: [Plan of Cuernavaca](/wiki/Plan_of_Cuernavaca "Plan of Cuernavaca")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9b/Valent%C3%ADn_G%C3%B3mez_Far%C3%ADas%2C_portrait.JPG/250px-Valent%C3%ADn_G%C3%B3mez_Far%C3%ADas%2C_portrait.JPG)](/wiki/File:Valent%C3%ADn_G%C3%B3mez_Far%C3%ADas,_portrait.JPG)

Dr. [Valentín Gómez Farías](/wiki/Valent%C3%ADn_G%C3%B3mez_Far%C3%ADas "Valentín Gómez Farías"), Santa Anna's vice president 1833–34, who enacted liberal reforms

Santa Anna was elected president on 1 April 1833, but while he desired the title, he was not interested in governing. According to Mexican historian [Enrique Krauze](/wiki/Enrique_Krauze "Enrique Krauze"), "It annoyed him and bored him, and perhaps frightened him."[[35]](#cite_note-35) A biographer of Santa Anna describes his role during this period as the "absentee president".[[36]](#cite_note-36) Vice-president [Valentín Gómez Farías](/wiki/Valent%C3%ADn_G%C3%B3mez_Far%C3%ADas "Valentín Gómez Farías") took over the responsibility of governing the nation while Santa Anna retired to [Manga de Clavo](/wiki/Manga_de_Clavo "Manga de Clavo"), his *[hacienda](/wiki/Hacienda "Hacienda")* in Veracruz. Gómez Farías was a moderate, but he had a radical liberal congress with which to contend, perhaps a reason that Santa Anna left executive power to him.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

Mexico was faced with an empty treasury and an 11 million peso debt incurred by the Bustamante government. Gómez Farías could not cut back on the bloated expenditures on the army and sought other revenues. Taking a chapter out of the late Bourbon Reforms, he targeted the [Roman Catholic Church](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Church "Roman Catholic Church"). Anticlericalism was a tenet of [Mexican liberalism](/wiki/Liberalism_in_Mexico "Liberalism in Mexico"), and the church had supported Bustamante's government, so targeting that institution was a logical move. [Tithing](/wiki/Tithing "Tithing") (a 10% tax on agricultural production) was abolished as a legal obligation, and church property and finances were seized. The church's role in education was reduced and the [Royal and Pontifical University of Mexico](/wiki/Royal_and_Pontifical_University_of_Mexico "Royal and Pontifical University of Mexico") closed. All this caused concern among Mexican conservatives.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

Gómez Farías sought to extend these reforms to the frontier province of [Alta California](/wiki/Alta_California "Alta California"), promoting legislation to [secularize](/wiki/Spanish_missions_in_California#Secularization "Spanish missions in California") the [Franciscan](/wiki/Franciscans "Franciscans") missions there. In 1833 he organized the Híjar-Padrés colony to bolster non-mission civilian settlement, as well as defend the province against perceived [Russian](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire") colonial ambitions from the trading post at [Fort Ross](/wiki/Fort_Ross,_California "Fort Ross, California").[[39]](#cite_note-39) However, for liberal intellectual and Catholic priest [José María Luis Mora](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Mar%C3%ADa_Luis_Mora "José María Luis Mora"), selling church property was the key to "transforming Mexico into a liberal, progressive nation of small landowners." Sale of nonessential church property would bring in much-needed revenue to the treasury. The army was also targeted for reform, since it was the largest single expenditure in the national budget. On Santa Anna's suggestion, the number of [battalions](/wiki/Battalion "Battalion") was to be reduced as well as the number of generals and brigadiers.[[40]](#cite_note-40)

The government soon issued a law, the *Ley del Caso,* which called for the arrest of 51 politicians, including Bustamante, for holding "unpatriotic" beliefs and their expulsion from the country. Gómez Farías claimed that Santa Anna was the driving force for the law, which evidence seems to support.[[41]](#cite_note-41) With increasing resistance from the church as well as the army, the Plan of Cuernavaca was issued, likely orchestrated by former general and governor of the Federal District, [José María Tornel](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Mar%C3%ADa_Tornel "José María Tornel"). The plan called for repeal of the *Ley del Caso*; discouraged tolerance of the influence of [Masonic lodges](/wiki/Masonic_lodge "Masonic lodge"), where politics was pursued in secrecy; declared void the laws passed by [Congress](/wiki/Congress_of_the_Union "Congress of the Union") and the local legislatures in favor of the reforms; requested the protection of Santa Anna to fulfill the plan and recognize him as the only authority; removed from office deputies and officials who carried out enforcement of the reform laws and decrees; and provided military force to support Gómez Farías in implementing the plan.[[42]](#cite_note-42)

As opinion turned against the reforms, Santa Anna was persuaded to return to the presidency and Gómez Farías resigned. This set the stage for conservatives to reshape Mexico's government from a federalist republic to a unitary central republic.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

### Central Republic, 1835

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Antonio_L%C3%B3pez_de_Santa_Anna&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Central Republic, 1835")]

Main article: [Centralist Republic of Mexico](/wiki/Centralist_Republic_of_Mexico "Centralist Republic of Mexico")

Further information: [Siete Leyes](/wiki/Siete_Leyes "Siete Leyes") and [Rebellion in Zacatecas](/wiki/Rebellion_in_Zacatecas "Rebellion in Zacatecas")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/Santaanna1.JPG/330px-Santaanna1.JPG)](/wiki/File:Santaanna1.JPG)

Santa Anna in a Mexican military uniform

For conservatives, the liberal reform of Gómez Farías was radical and threatened the power of the elites. Santa Anna's actions in allowing this first reform (followed by a more sweeping one in 1855) might have been a test case for liberalism. At this point, Santa Anna was a liberal; by giving the moderate Gómez Farías responsibility for the reforms, he could have [plausible deniability](/wiki/Plausible_deniability "Plausible deniability") and closely monitor the reaction to a comprehensive attack on the special privileges of the army and the church, as well as confiscation of church wealth, enacted by Congress.

In May 1834, Santa Anna ordered the disarmament of the civic militia and urged Congress to abolish the controversial *Ley del Caso*.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGonzález_Pedrero2004468-44) On 12 June he dissolved Congress and announced his decision to adopt the Plan of Cuernavaca, forming a new Catholic, centralist and conservative government.[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOlavarría_y_Ferrari1880344-45) Santa Anna brokered a deal where, in exchange for preserving the privileges of the church and the army, the church promised a monthly donation to the government of 30,000–40,000 pesos.[[46]](#cite_note-46) "The santanistas [supporters of Santa Anna] succeeded in achieving what the radicals had failed to do: forcing the Church to assist the republic's daily fiscal needs with its funds and properties."[[47]](#cite_note-47)

On 4 January 1835, Santa Anna returned to his *hacienda*, placing [Miguel Barragán](/wiki/Miguel_Barrag%C3%A1n "Miguel Barragán") as acting president. He soon replaced the 1824 constitution with the new document known as the "[Siete Leyes](/wiki/Siete_Leyes "Siete Leyes")" ("The Seven Laws"). Santa Anna did not involve himself with the conservative effort to replace the federalist constitution with a unitary central government, seemingly uneasy with their political path. "Although he has been blamed for the change to centralism, he was not actually present during any of the deliberations that led to the abolition of the federalist charter or the elaboration of the 1836 Constitution."[[48]](#cite_note-48)[[49]](#cite_note-49)

Several states openly rebelled against the changes, including Alta California, [Nuevo México](/wiki/Santa_Fe_de_Nuevo_M%C3%A9xico "Santa Fe de Nuevo México"), [Tabasco](/wiki/Tabasco "Tabasco"), [Sonora](/wiki/Sonora "Sonora"), [Coahuila y Tejas](/wiki/Coahuila_y_Tejas "Coahuila y Tejas"), San Luis Potosí, Querétaro, [Durango](/wiki/Durango "Durango"), Guanajuato, Michoacán, Yucatán, [Jalisco](/wiki/Jalisco "Jalisco"), [Nuevo León](/wiki/Nuevo_Le%C3%B3n "Nuevo León"), [Tamaulipas](/wiki/Tamaulipas "Tamaulipas"), and [Zacatecas](/wiki/Zacatecas "Zacatecas"). Several of these states formed their own governments: the [Republic of the Rio Grande](/wiki/Republic_of_the_Rio_Grande "Republic of the Rio Grande"), the [Republic of Yucatán](/wiki/Republic_of_Yucat%C3%A1n "Republic of Yucatán"), and the [Republic of Texas](/wiki/Republic_of_Texas "Republic of Texas"). Their fierce resistance was possibly fueled by Santa Anna's [reprisals](/wiki/Reprisal "Reprisal") committed against his defeated enemies.[[50]](#cite_note-50) The *[New York Post](/wiki/New_York_Post "New York Post")* editorialized that "had Santa Anna treated the vanquished with moderation and generosity, it would have been difficult if not impossible to awaken that general sympathy for the people of Texas which now impels so many adventurous and ardent spirits to throng to the aid of their brethren."[[51]](#cite_note-lord169-51)

The Zacatecas militia, the largest and best supplied of the Mexican states, led by [Francisco García Salinas](/wiki/Francisco_Garc%C3%ADa_Salinas "Francisco García Salinas"), was well armed with .753 caliber British '[Brown Bess](/wiki/Brown_Bess "Brown Bess")' [muskets](/wiki/Musket "Musket") and Baker .61 [rifles](/wiki/Rifle "Rifle"). But, after two hours of combat on 12 May 1835, Santa Anna's "Army of Operations" [defeated the Zacatecan militia](/wiki/Battle_of_Zacatecas_(1835) "Battle of Zacatecas (1835)") and took almost 3,000 prisoners. He allowed his army to loot [Zacatecas City](/wiki/Zacatecas_City "Zacatecas City") for forty-eight hours. After conquering Zacatecas, he planned to move on to Coahuila y Tejas to quell the rebellion there, which was being supported by [settlers from the United States](/wiki/Texians "Texians").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Texas Revolution 1835–1836

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Antonio_L%C3%B3pez_de_Santa_Anna&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Texas Revolution 1835–1836")]

Further information: [Texas Revolution](/wiki/Texas_Revolution "Texas Revolution")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/de/FalloftheAlamo.jpg/250px-FalloftheAlamo.jpg)](/wiki/File:FalloftheAlamo.jpg)

Fall of the Alamo to Santa Anna's forces, 6 March 1836

In 1835, Santa Anna repealed the Mexican constitution, which ultimately led to the beginning of the Texas Revolution. His reasoning for the repeal was that American settlers in Texas were not paying taxes or [tariffs](/wiki/Tariff "Tariff"), claiming they were not recipients of any services provided by the Mexican government; as a result, new settlers were not allowed there. The new policy was a response to the U.S. attempts to purchase Texas from Mexico.[[52]](#cite_note-Wright-52) Like other states discontented with the central government, the Texas Department of the Mexican state of Coahuila y Tejas rebelled in late 1835 and declared itself independent on 2 March 1836. The northeastern part of the state had been settled by numerous American immigrants. [Moses Austin](/wiki/Moses_Austin "Moses Austin"), the father of [Stephen F. Austin](/wiki/Stephen_F._Austin "Stephen F. Austin"), had his party accepted by Spanish authorities in exchange for defense against foreign threats. However, Mexico had declared independence from Spain before the elder Austin died.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Santa Anna marched north to bring Texas back under Mexican control by a brutal [show of force](/wiki/Show_of_force "Show of force"). His expedition posed challenges of manpower, logistics, supply and strategy far beyond what he was prepared for, and it ended in disaster. To fund, organize and equip his army, Santa Anna relied, as he often did, on forcing wealthy men to "loan" him funds. He recruited hastily, sweeping up many derelicts and ex-convicts, as well as [Indians](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Indigenous peoples of the Americas") who could not understand Spanish commands.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Having expected tropical weather, Santa Anna's army suffered from cold, a lack of proper clothing and food shortages. Stretching a supply line far longer than ever before, there were not enough horses, mules, cattle and wagons available, resulting in units never having enough food, fuel, or feed. The medical facilities were minimal and poorly supplied. Morale sank as soldiers realized there were not enough [chaplains](/wiki/Chaplain "Chaplain") to properly bury their bodies. Hostile Indians picked off stragglers and foragers. Waterborne sicknesses spread quickly when the men were forced to drink any water they could find on the trail. The officers proved to be mostly incompetent, yet the highly insulated and rigid hierarchy of the army meant that Santa Anna was kept ignorant of these problems.[[53]](#cite_note-53)

Santa Anna's forces killed 189 Texan defenders at the [Battle of the Alamo](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Alamo "Battle of the Alamo") on 6 March 1836, and executed more than 342 Texan prisoners at the [Goliad Massacre](/wiki/Goliad_Massacre "Goliad Massacre") on 27 March 1836. However, his forces suffered unexpectedly heavy casualties. In an 1874 letter, Santa Anna asserted that killing the defenders of Alamo was his only option, stressing that Texan commander [William B. Travis](/wiki/William_B._Travis "William B. Travis") was to blame for the degree of violence during the battle. Santa Anna believed that Travis was disrespectful towards him, and that if he had spared the Texans, it would have allowed [Sam Houston](/wiki/Sam_Houston "Sam Houston") to establish a dominant position that could threaten him later.[[54]](#cite_note-54)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/SantaAnnaSurrender.jpg/250px-SantaAnnaSurrender.jpg)](/wiki/File:SantaAnnaSurrender.jpg)

*"Surrender of Santa Anna"* by [William Henry Huddle](/wiki/William_Henry_Huddle "William Henry Huddle") shows the Mexican president and general surrendering to a wounded Sam Houston, [battle of San Jacinto](/wiki/Battle_of_San_Jacinto "Battle of San Jacinto")

The Mexican victory at the Alamo bought time for Houston and his Texas forces. During the siege, the [Texian Navy](/wiki/Texian_Navy "Texian Navy") had more time to plunder ports along the Gulf of Mexico, and the [Texian Army](/wiki/Texian_Army "Texian Army") gained more experience and weaponry. Despite Houston's lack of ability to maintain strict control of the Army, they completely routed Santa Anna's much larger army at the [Battle of San Jacinto](/wiki/Battle_of_San_Jacinto "Battle of San Jacinto") on 21 April 1836. The day after the battle, a small Texan force led by [James Austin Sylvester](/wiki/James_Austin_Sylvester "James Austin Sylvester") captured Santa Anna near a marsh; the general had hastily dressed himself in a dead Mexican dragoon's uniform but was quickly recognized.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

After three weeks in captivity,[[55]](#cite_note-55) [Texas President](/wiki/President_of_Texas "President of Texas") [David G. Burnet](/wiki/David_G._Burnet "David G. Burnet") and Santa Anna signed the [Treaties of Velasco](/wiki/Treaties_of_Velasco "Treaties of Velasco") stating that "in his official character as chief of the Mexican nation, he acknowledged the full, entire, and perfect Independence of the Republic of Texas." In exchange, Burnet and the Texas government guaranteed Santa Anna's safety and transport to Veracruz. Meanwhile, in Mexico City, a new government declared that Santa Anna was no longer president and that the Treaties were null and void. While Santa Anna was held captive in Texas, Poinsett offered a harsh assessment of his situation: "Say to General Santa Anna that when I remember how ardent an advocate he was of liberty ten years ago, I have no sympathy for him now, that he has gotten what he deserves." Santa Anna replied: "Say to Mr. Poinsett that it is very true that I threw up my cap for liberty with great ardor, and perfect sincerity, but very soon found the folly of it. A hundred years to come my people will not be fit for liberty. They do not know what it is, unenlightened as they are, and under the influence of Catholic clergy, a despotism is a proper government for them, but there is no reason why it should not be a wise and virtuous one."[[56]](#cite_note-56)

### Redemption, dictatorship, and exile

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Antonio_L%C3%B3pez_de_Santa_Anna&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Redemption, dictatorship, and exile")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/%C3%89pisode_de_l%27exp%C3%A9dition_du_Mexique_en_1838_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-%C3%89pisode_de_l%27exp%C3%A9dition_du_Mexique_en_1838_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:%C3%89pisode_de_l%27exp%C3%A9dition_du_Mexique_en_1838_(cropped).jpg)

French bombardment of the fort of San Juan de Ulúa in the [Pastry War](/wiki/Pastry_War "Pastry War")

After some time in exile, and after meeting [U.S. President](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States") [Andrew Jackson](/wiki/Andrew_Jackson "Andrew Jackson") in 1837, Santa Anna was allowed to return to Mexico. He was transported aboard the [USS *Pioneer*](/wiki/USS_Pioneer_(1836) "USS Pioneer (1836)") to retire to his *hacienda* in Veracruz. There he wrote a manifesto in which he reflected on his experiences and decision-making in Texas.[[57]](#cite_note-57)[[58]](#cite_note-58)

In 1838, Santa Anna found a chance for redemption from the loss of Texas. After Mexico rejected demands for financial compensation for losses suffered by its citizens, [France](/wiki/July_Monarchy "July Monarchy") sent forces that landed in Veracruz in the [Pastry War](/wiki/Pastry_War "Pastry War"). The Mexican government gave Santa Anna control of the army and ordered him to defend the nation by any means necessary. Santa Anna engaged the French at Veracruz but was forced to retreat after a failed assault, sustaining injuries in his left leg and hand by cannon fire. His shattered ankle required [amputation](/wiki/Amputation "Amputation") of much of his leg, which he ordered buried with full military honors.[[59]](#cite_note-59) Despite Mexico's final capitulation to French demands, Santa Anna used his war service and visible sacrifice to the nation to re-enter Mexican politics.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/Combat_de_Vera_Cruz_1838_Prince_de_Joinville_attaque_la_maison_du_general_Arista.jpg/250px-Combat_de_Vera_Cruz_1838_Prince_de_Joinville_attaque_la_maison_du_general_Arista.jpg)](/wiki/File:Combat_de_Vera_Cruz_1838_Prince_de_Joinville_attaque_la_maison_du_general_Arista.jpg)

Santa Anna was severely wounded and narrowly escaped capture in the French attack on Veracruz in 1838.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/23/03_Antonio_L%C3%B3pez_de_Santa_Anna.jpg/250px-03_Antonio_L%C3%B3pez_de_Santa_Anna.jpg)](/wiki/File:03_Antonio_L%C3%B3pez_de_Santa_Anna.jpg)

Antonio López de Santa Anna.

Soon after, with Bustamante's presidency descending into chaos, supporters asked Santa Anna to take control of the provisional government. Santa Anna was made president for the fifth time, taking over a nation with an empty treasury. The war with France had weakened the country, and the people were discontented. Also, a rebel army led by Generals [José de Urrea](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_de_Urrea "José de Urrea") and [José Antonio Mexía](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Antonio_Mex%C3%ADa "José Antonio Mexía"), was marching towards Mexico City in opposition to Santa Anna. Commanding the army, Santa Anna crushed the rebellion in [Puebla](/wiki/Puebla "Puebla").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Santa Anna ruled in a more [dictatorial](/wiki/Dictator "Dictator") fashion than during his first administration. His government banned anti-Santanista newspapers and jailed [dissidents](/wiki/Dissident "Dissident") to suppress opposition. In 1842, he directed a [military expedition into Texas](/wiki/Dawson_massacre "Dawson massacre"). The action inflicted numerous casualties with no political gain, but Texans began to be persuaded of the potential benefits of [annexation](/wiki/Texas_annexation "Texas annexation") by the more powerful U.S.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Following the 1842 elections, at which a new Congress was elected which opposed his rule,[[60]](#cite_note-60) Santa Anna attempted to restore the treasury by raising taxes. Several Mexican states stopped dealing with the central government in response, and Yucatán and Laredo declared themselves independent republics. With resentment growing, Santa Anna stepped down and fled Mexico City in December 1844. The buried leg he left behind in the capital was dug up by a mob and dragged through the streets until nothing was left of it.[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECamnitzer2009-61)[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFowler2009239-62) Fearing for his life, Santa Anna tried to elude capture, but in January 1845 he was apprehended by a group of Native Americans near [Xico](/wiki/Xico,_Veracruz "Xico, Veracruz"). They turned him over to authorities, and he was imprisoned. Santa Anna's life was ultimately spared, but he was exiled to Cuba.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Mexican–American War, 1846–1848

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Antonio_L%C3%B3pez_de_Santa_Anna&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Mexican–American War, 1846–1848")]

Main article: [Mexican–American War](/wiki/Mexican%E2%80%93American_War "Mexican–American War")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4b/Battle-of-Buena-Vista-Robinson.jpeg/250px-Battle-of-Buena-Vista-Robinson.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Battle-of-Buena-Vista-Robinson.jpeg)

[Battle of Buena Vista](/wiki/Battle_of_Buena_Vista "Battle of Buena Vista")

In 1846, following American victories at [Palo Alto](/wiki/Battle_of_Palo_Alto "Battle of Palo Alto") and [Resaca de la Palma](/wiki/Battle_of_Resaca_de_la_Palma "Battle of Resaca de la Palma") in the Mexican-American War, President [Mariano Paredes](/wiki/Mariano_Paredes_(President_of_Mexico) "Mariano Paredes (President of Mexico)") was removed from office, with the new government seeking to reinstate the constitution of 1824, with Santa Anna again assuming the presidency. Santa Anna, who had been in exile for only a year, returned to Mexico on 6 August 1846, two days after Paredes' ouster. He wrote to the new government stating he had no aspirations to the presidency but would eagerly use his military experience in the new conflict with the U.S.

U.S. President [James K. Polk](/wiki/James_K._Polk "James K. Polk") had hoped to acquire territory in the north by purchase or force, but the Mexican government was not willing to yield. In a gambit to change the dynamic, Polk sent agents to secretly meet with the exiled Santa Anna. They thought they had extracted a promise from him that they would lift a [blockade](/wiki/Blockade "Blockade") of the Mexican coast to allow him to return and that he would broker a deal. Once back in Mexico at the head of an army, however, Santa Anna reneged on the deal and took up arms against the U.S. invasion.[[63]](#cite_note-63)

With no path now for a quick resolution to the conflict in the north, Polk authorized an invasion to take Mexico City, redirecting the bulk of General [Zachary Taylor](/wiki/Zachary_Taylor "Zachary Taylor")'s troops to General [Winfield Scott](/wiki/Winfield_Scott "Winfield Scott")'s army. Santa Anna mobilized troops and artillery and rapidly marched north. His forces outnumbered Taylor's, but his troops were exhausted, ill-clothed, hungry and equipped with inferior weapons when the two armies clashed at the [Battle of Buena Vista](/wiki/Battle_of_Buena_Vista "Battle of Buena Vista") on 22–23 February 1847. Hard fighting over two days brought an inconclusive result, with Santa Anna withdrawing from the field of battle overnight just as complete victory was at hand, taking war trophies such as cannons and [battle flags](/wiki/War_flag "War flag") as evidence of his victory. With Scott's army [landing at Veracruz](/wiki/Siege_of_Veracruz "Siege of Veracruz"), Santa Anna's home ground, he rapidly moved southward to engage with the invaders and protect the capital. For the Mexicans it would have been better if Scott could have been prevented from leaving the Gulf Coast, but they could not prevent Scott's march on [Xalapa](/wiki/Xalapa "Xalapa"). Santa Anna [set defenses at Cerro Gordo](/wiki/Battle_of_Cerro_Gordo "Battle of Cerro Gordo"). U.S. forces outflanked him and against strong odds defeated his army.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

With that battle, the way was clear for Scott's forces to advance further onto Mexico City. Santa Anna's aim was to protect the capital at all costs and waged defensive warfare, placing strong defenses on the most direct road into the city at El Peñon, which Scott then avoided. Battles at [Contreras](/wiki/Battle_of_Contreras "Battle of Contreras"), [Churubusco](/wiki/Battle_of_Churubusco "Battle of Churubusco"), and [Molino del Rey](/wiki/Battle_of_Molino_del_Rey "Battle of Molino del Rey") were lost. At Contreras, Mexican General [Gabriel Valencia](/wiki/Gabriel_Valencia "Gabriel Valencia"), an old political and military rival of Santa Anna's, did not recognize his authority as supreme commander and disobeyed his orders as to where his troops should be placed. Valencia's Army of the North was routed. The [Battle for Mexico City](/wiki/Battle_for_Mexico_City "Battle for Mexico City") and the [Battle of Chapultepec](/wiki/Battle_of_Chapultepec "Battle of Chapultepec"), like the others, were hard fought losses, and American forces took the capital. "Despite his many faults as a tactician and his overbearing political ambition, Santa Anna was committed to fighting to the bitter end. His actions would prolong the war for at least a year, and more than any other single person it was Santa Anna who denied Polk's dream of a short war."[[64]](#cite_note-64)

Perhaps Santa Anna's most personal and ignominious incident in the war was the capture during the [Battle of Cerro Gordo](/wiki/Battle_of_Cerro_Gordo "Battle of Cerro Gordo") of his prosthetic cork leg,[[65]](#cite_note-65) which remains as a war trophy in the U.S. held by the Illinois State Military Museum but no longer on display.[[66]](#cite_note-66) A second leg, a [peg](/wiki/Peg_leg "Peg leg"), was also captured by the [4th Illinois](/wiki/4th_Illinois_Volunteer_Infantry_Regiment "4th Illinois Volunteer Infantry Regiment") and was reportedly used by the soldiers as a baseball bat; it is displayed at the home of Illinois Governor [Richard J. Oglesby](/wiki/Richard_J._Oglesby "Richard J. Oglesby") (who served in the regiment) in [Decatur](/wiki/Decatur,_Illinois "Decatur, Illinois").[[67]](#cite_note-67) Santa Anna had a replacement leg made which is displayed at the Museo Nacional de Historia in Mexico City.[[68]](#cite_note-68)

The prosthetic leg later played a role in international politics. As relations between the U.S. and Mexico warmed during the run-up to [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), Illinois was rumored to be ready to return the prosthetic to Mexico and, in 1942, a bill was introduced in the state legislature. The Association of Limb Manufacturers wanted to be part of the [repatriation](/wiki/Repatriation "Repatriation") ceremonies. The state passed a non-binding resolution to return the prosthetic, but the [National Guard](/wiki/Illinois_National_Guard "Illinois National Guard") denied the transfer.[[69]](#cite_note-69) As of 2025 the leg still resided in the Illinois State Military Museum in [Springfield](/wiki/Springfield,_Illinois "Springfield, Illinois").[[70]](#cite_note-70)

### President for the last time, 1853–1855

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Antonio_L%C3%B3pez_de_Santa_Anna&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: President for the last time, 1853–1855")]

See also: [Second Mexican Republic](/wiki/Second_Mexican_Republic "Second Mexican Republic") and [Plan of Ayutla](/wiki/Plan_of_Ayutla "Plan of Ayutla")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/Gadsden_Purchase_Cities.svg/250px-Gadsden_Purchase_Cities.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Gadsden_Purchase_Cities.svg)

Gadsden Purchase of 1854, territory purchased by the U.S. for a better transcontinental railway route

Following Mexico's defeat in 1848, Santa Anna went into exile in [Kingston](/wiki/Kingston,_Jamaica "Kingston, Jamaica"), [Jamaica](/wiki/Colony_of_Jamaica "Colony of Jamaica"). Two years later, he moved to [Turbaco](/wiki/Turbaco "Turbaco") in [New Granada](/wiki/Republic_of_New_Granada "Republic of New Granada") (now Colombia). In April 1853, he was invited to return to Mexico by conservatives who had overthrown a weak liberal government, initiated under the Plan de Hospicio, drawn up by the clerics in the cathedral chapter of [Guadalajara](/wiki/Guadalajara "Guadalajara"). Usually, revolts were fomented by military officers; this one was fomented by churchmen.[[71]](#cite_note-71) Santa Anna was elected president on 17 March 1853. He honored his promises to the church, revoking a decree denying protection for the fulfillment of monastic vows, a reform promulgated twenty years earlier by Gómez Farías.[[72]](#cite_note-mecham-358-59-72) The [Jesuits](/wiki/Society_of_Jesus "Society of Jesus"), who had been expelled from Spanish realms by the crown in 1767, were allowed to return to Mexico ostensibly to educate poorer classes, and much of their property, which the crown had confiscated and sold, was restored to them.[[72]](#cite_note-mecham-358-59-72)

Although he gave himself exalted titles, Santa Anna's situation was quite vulnerable. He declared himself dictator-for-life with the title "Most Serene Highness". His full title in this final period of power was "Hero [*benemérito*] of the nation, General of Division, Grand Master of the National and Distinguished Order of Guadalupe, Grand Cross of the Royal and Distinguished Spanish Order of Carlos III, and President of the Mexican Republic."[[73]](#cite_note-73) The reality was that this administration was no more successful than his earlier ones, dependent on loans from moneylenders and support from conservative elites, the church, and the army.

A major miscalculation was Santa Anna's sale of territory to the U.S. in what became known as the [Gadsden Purchase](/wiki/Gadsden_Purchase "Gadsden Purchase"). *La Mesilla*, the land in northwest Mexico that the U.S. wanted, was much easier terrain for the building of a [transcontinental railway](/wiki/Transcontinental_railroad "Transcontinental railroad") in the U.S. The purchase money for the land was supposedly to go to Mexico's empty treasury. Santa Anna was unwilling to wait until the final transaction went through and the boundary line established, wanting access to the money immediately. He bargained with American bankers to get immediate cash, while they gained the right to the revenue when the sale closed. Santa Anna's short-sighted deal netted the Mexican government only $250,000 against credit of $650,000 going to the bankers. [James Gadsden](/wiki/James_Gadsden "James Gadsden") thought the amount was likely much higher.[[74]](#cite_note-74) A group of liberals including Alvarez, [Benito Juárez](/wiki/Benito_Ju%C3%A1rez "Benito Juárez"), and [Ignacio Comonfort](/wiki/Ignacio_Comonfort "Ignacio Comonfort") overthrew Santa Anna under the Plan of Ayutla, which called for his removal from office. He went into exile yet again in 1855.

By the [Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo](/wiki/Treaty_of_Guadalupe_Hidalgo "Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo") the United States paid Mexico only $15 million for the land, which became known as the Mexican Cession.

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Antonio_L%C3%B3pez_de_Santa_Anna&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Personal life")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/Juan_Cordero_-_Portrait_of_Do%C3%B1a_Dolores_Tosta_de_Santa_Anna_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg/250px-Juan_Cordero_-_Portrait_of_Do%C3%B1a_Dolores_Tosta_de_Santa_Anna_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)](/wiki/File:Juan_Cordero_-_Portrait_of_Do%C3%B1a_Dolores_Tosta_de_Santa_Anna_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)

*Portrait of Doña Dolores Tosta de Santa Anna* by [Juan Cordero](/wiki/Juan_Cordero "Juan Cordero"), 1855. Note her tiara. Santa Anna was considered by some an uncrowned monarch of Mexico.

Santa Anna married twice, both times to wealthy young women. At neither wedding ceremony did he appear, legally empowering his future father-in-law to serve as a proxy at his first wedding and a friend at his second.[[75]](#cite_note-75) One assessment of the two marriages is that they were [arranged marriages](/wiki/Arranged_marriage "Arranged marriage") of convenience, bringing considerable wealth to Santa Anna and that his lack of attendance at the ceremonies "appears to confirm that he was purely interested in the financial aspect o[f] the alliance."[[76]](#cite_note-fowler58-76)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/Johhan_Moritz_Rugendas%2C_Manga_de_Clavo._Hacienda_von_General_Santa_Anna.jpg/250px-Johhan_Moritz_Rugendas%2C_Manga_de_Clavo._Hacienda_von_General_Santa_Anna.jpg)](/wiki/File:Johhan_Moritz_Rugendas,_Manga_de_Clavo._Hacienda_von_General_Santa_Anna.jpg)

Santa Anna's first and favorite hacienda [Manga de Clavo](/wiki/Manga_de_Clavo "Manga de Clavo"), which his first wife's dowry enabled him to purchase. Painting by [Johann Moritz Rugendas](/wiki/Johann_Moritz_Rugendas "Johann Moritz Rugendas"). Kuperferstichkabinett, Staatliche Museen Zu Berlin, Id. Number: VIII E. 2440, 1831–1834.

In 1825, Santa Anna married [Inés García](/wiki/In%C3%A9s_Garc%C3%ADa_de_L%C3%B3pez_de_Santa_Anna "Inés García de López de Santa Anna"), the daughter of wealthy Spanish parents in Veracruz, and the couple had four children: María de Guadalupe, María del Carmen, Manuel, and Antonio López de Santa Anna y García.[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFowler200992-77) By 1825, Santa Anna had distinguished himself as a military man, joining the movement for independence. When Iturbide lost support, Santa Anna had been in the forefront of leaders seeking to oust him. Although his family was of modest means, Santa Anna was of good creole lineage; the García family may well have seen a match between their young daughter and the up-and-coming Santa Anna as advantageous. Inés' [dowry](/wiki/Dowry "Dowry") allowed Santa Anna to purchase the first of his haciendas, Manga de Clavo, in Veracruz.[[76]](#cite_note-fowler58-76)[[78]](#cite_note-78)

The first Spanish ambassador to Mexico and his wife, [Fanny Calderón de la Barca](/wiki/Frances_Erskine_Inglis,_1st_Marquise_of_Calder%C3%B3n_de_la_Barca "Frances Erskine Inglis, 1st Marquise of Calderón de la Barca"), visited with Inés at Manga de Clavo, where they were well-received with a breakfast banquet. Calderón de la Barca observed that "After breakfast, the Señora having dispatched an officer for her cigar-case, which was gold with a diamond latch, offered me a cigar, which I having declined, she lighted her own, a little paper 'cigarette', and the gentlemen followed her good example."[[79]](#cite_note-79)

Two months after the death of his wife Inés in 1844, the 50-year-old Santa Anna married 16-year-old María de los Dolores de Tosta. The couple rarely lived together; De Tosta resided primarily in Mexico City, and Santa Anna's political and military activities took him around the country.[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFowler2009229-80) They had no children, leading biographer Will Fowler to speculate that either the marriage was primarily platonic or De Tosta was infertile.[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFowler2009229-80)

Several women claimed to have borne Santa Anna [natural](/wiki/Legitimacy_(family_law) "Legitimacy (family law)") children. In his will, he acknowledged and made provisions for four: Paula, María de la Merced, Petra, and José López de Santa Anna. Biographers have identified three more: Pedro López de Santa Anna, and Ángel and Augustina Rosa López de Santa Anna.[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFowler200992-77)

## Later years and death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Antonio_L%C3%B3pez_de_Santa_Anna&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Later years and death")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/Antonio_Lopez_de_Santa_Anna_c1870.jpg/250px-Antonio_Lopez_de_Santa_Anna_c1870.jpg)](/wiki/File:Antonio_Lopez_de_Santa_Anna_c1870.jpg)

Santa Anna photographed by [George G. Rockwood](/wiki/George_G._Rockwood "George G. Rockwood"), c.1870

From 1855 to 1874, Santa Anna lived in exile in Cuba, the United States, Colombia, and [Saint Thomas](/wiki/Saint_Thomas,_U.S._Virgin_Islands "Saint Thomas, U.S. Virgin Islands"). He had left Mexico because of his unpopularity with the Mexican people after his defeat in 1848. Santa Anna participated in [gambling](/wiki/Gambling "Gambling") and businesses with the hopes that he would become rich. During his many years in exile, he was a passionate fan of the sport of [cockfighting](/wiki/Cockfighting "Cockfighting"); he had many roosters that he entered into competitions and would have his roosters compete with cocks from all over the world.[[81]](#cite_note-:0-81)

In the 1850s, Santa Anna traveled to [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") with a shipment of [chicle](/wiki/Chicle "Chicle"), which he intended to sell for use in [making carriage wheels](/wiki/Wheelwright "Wheelwright"). He attempted but was unsuccessful in convincing U.S. wheel manufacturers that this substance could be more useful in tires than the materials they were originally using. Although he introduced chewing gum to the U.S., Santa Anna did not make any money from the product.[[81]](#cite_note-:0-81) [Thomas Adams](/wiki/Thomas_Adams_(chewing_gum_maker) "Thomas Adams (chewing gum maker)"), the American assigned to aid Santa Anna while he was in the U.S., experimented with chicle in an attempt to use it as a substitute for rubber. He bought one ton of the substance from Santa Anna, but his experiments proved unsuccessful. Instead, Adams helped to found the chewing gum industry with a product that he called "[chiclets](/wiki/Chiclets "Chiclets")".[[82]](#cite_note-Staten-82)

In 1865, Santa Anna attempted to return to Mexico and offer his services during the [French invasion](/wiki/Second_French_intervention_in_Mexico "Second French intervention in Mexico"), seeking once again to play the role as the country's defender and savior, only to be refused by Juárez. Later that year a [schooner](/wiki/Schooner "Schooner") owned by Gilbert Thompson, son-in-law of [Daniel Tompkins](/wiki/Daniel_D._Tompkins "Daniel D. Tompkins"), brought Santa Anna to his home in [Staten Island](/wiki/Staten_Island "Staten Island"),[[83]](#cite_note-83) where he tried to raise money for an army to return and take over Mexico City.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/da/Casa_de_Antonio_L%C3%B3pez_de_Santa_Anna.JPG/250px-Casa_de_Antonio_L%C3%B3pez_de_Santa_Anna.JPG)](/wiki/File:Casa_de_Antonio_L%C3%B3pez_de_Santa_Anna.JPG)

The house in [Mexico City](/wiki/Mexico_City "Mexico City") where Santa Anna spent the last years of his life and wrote most of his memoirs.

In 1874, Santa Anna took advantage of a general [amnesty](/wiki/Amnesty "Amnesty") issued by President [Sebastián Lerdo de Tejada](/wiki/Sebasti%C3%A1n_Lerdo_de_Tejada "Sebastián Lerdo de Tejada") and returned to Mexico, by then crippled and almost blind from [cataracts](/wiki/Cataract "Cataract"). He died at his home in Mexico City on 21 June 1876 at age 82. Santa Anna was buried with full military honors in a glass coffin in Panteón del Tepeyac Cemetery.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Antonio_L%C3%B3pez_de_Santa_Anna&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Legacy")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This section **may be [unbalanced](/wiki/Wikipedia:DUE "Wikipedia:DUE") towards certain viewpoints**. Please [improve the article](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Antonio_L%C3%B3pez_de_Santa_Anna&action=edit) or discuss the issue on the [talk page](/wiki/Talk:Antonio_L%C3%B3pez_de_Santa_Anna "Talk:Antonio López de Santa Anna"). *(January 2026)* |

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c3/Tumbasantaanna.jpg/250px-Tumbasantaanna.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tumbasantaanna.jpg)

Grave of Santa Anna and his second wife, Sra. Dolores Tosta de Santa Anna

Santa Anna was highly controversial at the time and ever since. In the 2007 biography by Will Fowler, he was depicted as, "a liberal, a Republican, an army man, a hero, a revolutionary, a regional strongman, but never a politician. He presented himself as a mediator who was both anti-party and anti-politics in the decades when the new country of Mexico was wracked by factional infighting. He was always more willing to lead an army than to lead his country".[[84]](#cite_note-84)

But as a military leader, Gates Brown, a historian at the [U.S. Army Command and General Staff College](/wiki/United_States_Army_Command_and_General_Staff_College "United States Army Command and General Staff College"), considers Santa Anna among history's worst for his mistakes in two wars which cost Mexico much of its territory. In the Texas Revolution, time was on his side at the Alamo since he knew the defenders were not being reinforced and would eventually have to surrender. Brown writes that he should have waited and built a logistical base at San Antonio to support further operations. Instead, he opted to attack after less than two weeks, losing more men than the defenders did, a large proportion of them experienced veterans. "He had sacrificed a third of his force", Brown writes, "to take a garrison that had to fall, with or without this assault." Outrage over the executions of Texans there and at Goliad built far more popular support for the rebellion than the Texans had themselves managed. Santa Anna's defeat and capture at San Jacinto was also abetted by his mistaken belief that Houston would not attack with a smaller force and troops as tired as his own.[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrown2022120–25-85)

Later, in the Mexican-American War, Santa Anna's decision to march newly recruited and inexperienced troops across 385 kilometres (239 mi) of desert in wintertime without stopping to resupply, in hopes of ending the war with a quick defeat of [Zachary Taylor](/wiki/Zachary_Taylor "Zachary Taylor")'s forces, contributed to the much greater Mexican casualty count at [Buena Vista](/wiki/Battle_of_Buena_Vista "Battle of Buena Vista"). At Cerro Gordo he dismissed suggestions from [Manuel Robles Pezuela](/wiki/Manuel_Robles_Pezuela "Manuel Robles Pezuela"), one of his officers, that he reinforce the Atalaya hill's defenses, believing the terrain made that unnecessary. The U.S. attack up that hill the next day, combined with a flanking maneuver, cost Mexico its only chance to halt General [Winfield Scott](/wiki/Winfield_Scott "Winfield Scott")'s advance on Mexico City before the outskirts of the city itself.[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrown2022126–27-86)

Historians note that Antonio López de Santa Anna became prominent in [Tejano](/wiki/Tejano "Tejano") folk memory because of his role in the Texas Revolution and the Battle of the Alamo. Later generations would reshape his image to serve cultural and political narratives. Texas educators, writers, and public officials throughout the nineteenth and twentieth centuries portrayed Santa Anna as the central antagonist in the broader story of how Texas gained independence. Historian Will Fowler says that this retrospective framing of Santa Anna contributes to exaggerated personal influence, transforming Santa Anna into a symbol of Mexican autocracy, rather than a complex political figure navigating Mexican internal instability during the nineteenth century.[[87]](#cite_note-:2-87)

In his dissertation *Inventing the Alamo*, Stephen Oleszek argues that Santa Anna’s rise as a Texas [folk legend](/wiki/Folk_legend "Folk legend") was closely related to the myth-making and storytelling process that surrounds the Alamo. Oleszek explains that in early Texas, memorial associations and historians sought to establish a distinct hero-villain[[88]](#cite_note-:4-88) identity between Texas and Mexico to promote unity amongst Anglo-Americans and to justify the memorialization of the Texas Revolution. Specifically, Santa Anna was portrayed as the embodiment of despotism, while Alamo defenders such as William B. Travis and [James Bowie](/wiki/James_Bowie "James Bowie") were portrayed as heroic and brave.[[89]](#cite_note-:1-89) This theme has been ingrained throughout textbooks, media, and the slogan, "Remember the Alamo!", which have presented an oversimplified narrative to generations of Texans.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

More recent works, specifically *Santa Anna in Texas: A Mexican American Viewpoint*, challenge these ideas by examining the military and political aspect of Santa Anna’s strategies to suppress the Texian Army in 1836. Presley says that Mexican perspectives on Santa Anna were actually divided, and in most cases, did not align with the villainous narrative found in Texan historiography.[[90]](#cite_note-:3-90) In the twenty-first century, historians emphasize the importance of the difference between cultural memory and historical events that are built around Santa Anna’s reputation which arose from being just a historical figure into what would later be a Battle of the Alamo legend known for his infamous acts in the Texas Revolution.[*[clarify](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*][[91]](#cite_note-91)

## In popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Antonio_L%C3%B3pez_de_Santa_Anna&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: In popular culture")]

* He features in several 19th-century-origin British sea shanties, including [one frequently known as "Santianna", "Santy Anno", or many other names](/wiki/Santianna "Santianna"), which has been frequently recorded by many folk musicians.
* He is played by Rubén Padilla (Mexican actor, not to be confused with the homonymous American athlete) in the John Wayne film *[The Alamo](/wiki/The_Alamo_(1960_film) "The Alamo (1960 film)")*.
* Fox animated series *[King of the Hill](/wiki/King_of_the_Hill "King of the Hill")* season 2 episode 18 "The Final Shinsult" largely revolves around Santa Anna's prosthetic leg.
* In the 1998 film *[The Mask of Zorro](/wiki/The_Mask_of_Zorro "The Mask of Zorro")*, Santa Anna is mentioned and is portrayed by [Joaquim de Almeida](/wiki/Joaquim_de_Almeida "Joaquim de Almeida") in an alternate ending.
* He is played by [Emilio Echevarría](/wiki/Emilio_Echevarr%C3%ADa "Emilio Echevarría") in the 2004 film *[The Alamo](/wiki/The_Alamo_(2004_film) "The Alamo (2004 film)")*.
* He is played by J. Carrol Naish in the 1955 film *[The Last Command](/wiki/The_Last_Command_(1955_film) "The Last Command (1955 film)")*.
* He appears in 2025's *[Civilization VII](/wiki/Civilization_VII "Civilization VII")* as one of Mexico's special Revolucionario Units.[[92]](#cite_note-92)
